source: Incus Server - 8 Container Snapshots: The snapshot process on the primary server

questions.q: Why are container snapshots so valuable (e.g. before an update)?
options: If the update breaks the app you restore the snapshot and are back up in seconds
questions.why: Snapshots give quick recovery -- ideal as a failsafe before updating software in a container.

questions.q: Can you snapshot a container while it is running?
questions.why: You can snapshot a running container.

questions.q: Create a snapshot named ubuntu-test-1 of the container ubuntu-test.
What is the safest way to restore a container from a snapshot?
options: Stop it, run incus snapshot restore <name> <snap>, then start it
questions.why: Stopping first is safest (especially after many changes), then restore, then start.

questions.q: Why does the book insist you delete snapshots while the container is RUNNING?
options: Because `incus delete <name>` deletes the whole container with no warning; a running container refuses that, protecting you
questions.why: A running container can't be deleted accidentally (it errors), so deleting snapshots while running guards against nuking the container.
The snapshots shown are stateless (they do not capture running memory state).
